coredata.dc:title: Sensitive and rapid detection of bacterial endotoxin with a functional carbon nanotube field-effect transistor biosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Qing
Li, Jiahao
Li, Yutao
Tang, Lina
Li, Kun
Li, Tingxian
Chen, Xiaohua
Zhang, Zhiyong
Zhang, Guo-Jun
coredata.dc:description: Endotoxin in the blood can cause unexplained fever, and even death due to endotoxemia and bacteremia. Rapid and sensitive detection of endotoxin has become a priority event to intervene in the occurrence of dangerous diseases in time. In this context, a carbon nanotubes-based field-effect transistor (CNTs FET) nanosensor is developed to realize the rapid, label-free and sensitive detection of endotoxin. The CNTs FET was fabricated by assembling the polymer-sorted high-purity semiconductor CNT films onto the sensing channel. In order to improve the detection sensitivity, carboxylated graphene quantum dots (cGQDs) were coupled to the CNT surface via the poly-l-lysine (PLL). After that, polymyxin B (PMB), which is highly specific for endotoxin, was covalently combined with cGQDs, thus enabling the capture and detection of endotoxin. The method not only displayed an extremely low level of limitation of detection in PBS (4.6 fg/mL) and serum (30.3 fg/mL), respectively, with superior resistance to interference, but also enabled the analysis of Gram-negative bacterial infections in blood samples in a short duration of time. Meanwhile, the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC) proved the superior diagnostic accuracy of this method (AUC = 0.990). Considering its excellent performance, the constructed CNTs FET biosensor is a promising tool to provide early warning of disease for clinical Gram-negative bacteremia and endotoxemia.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CNTs
FET biosensor
cGQDs
Endotoxin
Bacteremia

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of cGQD-modified CNTs FET devices
Covalent binding of PMB with cGQDs
Construction and characterization of cGQDs-functionalized CNTs FET
Sensitivity and selectivity of cGQDs-functionalized CNTs FET sensors
Stability of the PMB-functionalized CNTs FET sensors
Detection of endotoxin in simulation samples
Analysis of clinical samples using the PMB-modified CNTs FET
Real-time measurement of endotoxin in clinical samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21974035 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic diagram of the steps of LAL test for endotoxin detection. (B) Schematic diagram of the PMB-modified CNTs FET sensing platform for endotoxin detection.

Functionalization and characterization of PMB-modified CNTs FET sensing platform. (A) Functionalization process of sensing platform surface. (B) SEM images of CNTs FET sensor chips. (a) 6 sensing channels per chip. (b) Magnified SEM of one of the channels with CNTs connected to the source-drain. (c) Further magnified SEM with CNTs uniformly distributed. (C) The XPS of PLL before(green curve) and after(orange curve) deposition on CNTs. (D) TEM image of cGQDs. (E) The particle size distribution of cGQDs obtained from TEM. (F) Electrical characterization of each functionalization step, green line for original CNT, purple line for modification of PLL, orange line for modifying cGQDs, blue line for modifying PMB.

Analytical performance of PMB-modified CNTs FET. (A) Transfer characteristic curves obtained with cGQDs as linker molecules. (B) Calibration curves of a series of target ET concentrations (n = 3), dashed line indicates 3 signal-to-noise ratio. (C) Transfer characteristic curves after incubated with PBS, non-specific substances (including 100 pg/mL of β-glucan, d-galactose, l-cysteine, Glycine and ATP) and 100 fg/mL ET. (D) Changes of Ids in detecting blanks, 5 non-specific substances and 100 fg/mL ET.

Interference immunity of ΕΤ assay in serum. (A) Transfer characteristic curves and (B) variation of Ids (ΔIds) at a series range of target ET concentrations (100 fg/mL∼1 ng/mL) in undiluted serum samples from healthy individuals. (C) Direct comparison the result of the sensor in 1 × PBS and undiluted serum samples. (D) Real-time responses of three CNTs FET biosensors upon sequentially adding different concentrations of ET solution from 100 fg/mL to 1 ng/mL into undiluted serum at Vgs = −0.3 V and Vds = −0.1 V.

The PMB-modified CNTs FET nanosensor detect ET in serum samples from clinical Gram-negative bacterial infections. (A) Sensing signals of ET on 20 patients with Gram-negative bacterial infections and 10 healthy individuals. The illustration in the upper right corner illustrates the significant differences in signals between patients with gram-negative bacterial infections and healthy individuals. (B) Heat maps of the CNTs-FET, depicting the degree of signal shift for 30 clinical samples.” Pn” indicates Gram-negative bacterial infections samples (n = 1–20) and “Hn” indicates healthy samples (n = 1–10). (C) Correlation analysis of the CNTs-FET sensor with the LAL test results for each sample repeated 3 times. (D) ROC curves of PMB-CNTs FET nanosensor for detection of clinical Gram-negative bacterial infections samples.

Real-time electrical response of the PMB-modified CNTs FET sensor to serum from healthy individuals and patients with Gram-negative bacterial infections. (A) Schematic diagram of the real-time assay. (B–D) Results of the assay for the healthy sample and three different Gram-negative bacterial infected samples, inset: normalized current response induced by healthy and patient samples.

head.ce:title: Sensitive and rapid detection of bacterial endotoxin with a functional carbon nanotube field-effect transistor biosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for the Physics and Chemistry of Nanodevices and Center for Carbon-based Electronics, School of Electronics, Peking University, Beijing, 100871, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Laboratory Medicine, General Hospital of Central Theater Command, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: General Hospital of Central Theater Command

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Laboratory Medicine, General Hospital of Central Theater,Command, Wuhan, 430070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Endotoxin in the blood can cause unexplained fever, and even death due to endotoxemia and bacteremia. Rapid and sensitive detection of endotoxin has become a priority event to intervene in the occurrence of dangerous diseases in time. In this context, a carbon nanotubes-based field-effect transistor (CNTs FET) nanosensor is developed to realize the rapid, label-free and sensitive detection of endotoxin. The CNTs FET was fabricated by assembling the polymer-sorted high-purity semiconductor CNT films onto the sensing channel. In order to improve the detection sensitivity, carboxylated graphene quantum dots (cGQDs) were coupled to the CNT surface via the poly-l-lysine (PLL). After that, polymyxin B (PMB), which is highly specific for endotoxin, was covalently combined with cGQDs, thus enabling the capture and detection of endotoxin. The method not only displayed an extremely low level of limitation of detection in PBS (4.6 fg/mL) and serum (30.3 fg/mL), respectively, with superior resistance to interference, but also enabled the analysis of Gram-negative bacterial infections in blood samples in a short duration of time. Meanwhile, the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC) proved the superior diagnostic accuracy of this method (AUC = 0.990). Considering its excellent performance, the constructed CNTs FET biosensor is a promising tool to provide early warning of disease for clinical Gram-negative bacteremia and endotoxemia.

The cGQDs-functionalized CNT FET sensor is developed for sensitive and rapid detection of endotoxin.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: When the Gram-negative bacteria die in the development of bacteremia and endotoxemia [1], the endotoxin (ET), an indecomposable molecule, will be released to the human blood. Sub-nanomolar levels of ET in the blood can cause microcirculatory disorders and systemic inflammatory reactions [2,3], and even develop into endotoxemia, causing multi-organ dysfunction, infectious shock and death [4]. Meanwhile, ET is a biological indicator in the process of bacteremia [5]. Therefore, it is particularly important to detect ET sensitively and rapidly in the early stages of Gram-negative bacterial infections, which can provide early warning of bacteremia and endotoxemia of gram-negative bacterial infections, and has a powerful clinical importance.

The original ET detect method is the rabbit pyrogen test, which is progressively substituted by Limulus Amoebocyte lysate (LAL) test [6,7] due to the low specificity of the test [8,9] and the vulnerability of the test results to residual effects [10]. LAL test is the recommended method for ET detection and is also commercially available. However, this colorimetric or turbidimetric method is not sensitive enough [11], while limiting in application due to interfering molecules such as β-glucan [12]. In addition, obtaining horseshoe crab reagents requires the killing of massive quantities of scarce animals, leading to scarcity of test reagents [13].

As time passed, the spotlight was invested in searching for alternatives to LAL test, while ensuring the accurate and sensitive performance of the assay. One of the key steps is to design highly specific capture probes that selectively bind ET. Zhang [14] et al. constructed an affinity peptide-modified sensor chip, which was successfully applied to the detection of ET in injectable drugs. Cimen [15] et al. targeted molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) as the identifying element of ET, in which the ET in the concentration range of 0.5–100 ng/mL was successfully detected. Apart from that, aptamers [16,17] also have been applied to detect ET. Recently, polymyxin B (PMB) had been reported to have a high affinity to ET [18]. Compared to the affinity peptides, MIPs and aptamers, as mentioned above, PMB is a “natural probe” that does not require cumbersome synthesis steps and affinity screening, which is more economical. With its excellent performance, PMB has widely been used for ET detection. For example, as early as 2007, Ding [19] et al. investigated the binding effect between PMB and ET and successfully detected ET at concentrations of 0.2–0.8 ng/mL. Subsequently, Reta [20] et al. constructed a PMB-modified pSiM sensor with nanochannel arrays and detected ET in water samples with a LOD of 1.8 ng/mL. In addition, Tian [21] et al. developed a functionalized liposome with PMB as the recognition element, and successfully achieved the identification between Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria by the particular binding between PMB and ET. Most of these sensing platforms are based on the detection of ET in the environment, drugs and food, however, their sensitivity cannot either meet the clinical prevention and early diagnosis of endotoxemia, or achieve rapid detection of ET. Therefore, rapid and sensitive detection methods deserve further research.

Field-effect transistor (FET) biosensors, especially those modified with nanomaterials, due to their label-free and low-cost basis for ultra-sensitive detection of analytes [22,23], have become one of the most promising biosensors in recent years [24,25]. Among them, carbon nanotubes (CNTs)-based biosensors have enabled the detection of miRNA [26], proteins [27], DNA [28] and many other biomolecules. In order to attain a more satisfactory measurement performance, integration of nanoparticles on the surface of CNT can dramatically expand the specific surface area, thus boosting the catch effectiveness of targets. A large of studies of nanoparticles (iron oxide nanoparticles [29], Ag nanoparticles [30], gold nanoparticles [26–28], MnO2 nanoparticles [31]) modified on the surface of CNTs have also been reported. Among the various nanoparticles, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been widely applied by the merits of their small size, amphiphilic nature, easy of modification and superior conductivity [32]. For example, Hu et al. [33]. achieved the detection of miRNA-155 by constructing an electrochemical biosensor based on GQDs, in which GQDs served as a ligating platform to covalently link amino-modified DNA while non-covalently binding HRP, thereby enabling an enzyme-catalyzed electrochemical reaction. In our team, Li et al. [34] achieved the detection of miRNA 21 by construcing GQDs-based graphene FET biosensors. Here, PMO-GQDs hybrids were obtained by incubating phosphorodiamidate morpholino oligos (PMO) with GQDs to achieve successful immobilization of PMO on the sensor surface, which increases the detection sensitivity in a large part due to the three-dimensional structure of GQDs. Then, it is also desirable to construct GQDs-based CNT FET sensors for sensing detection.

Inspired by the above research background, in this work, as shown in Fig. 1 B, a carboxylated graphene quantum dots(cGQDs)-functionalized CNTs FET biosensor is reported for label-free and specific detection of ET by employing PMB as a probe. The CNTs FET is fabricated by assembling the polymer-sorted high-purity semiconductor CNT films onto the sensing channel of the fabricated FET. In order to improve the detection sensitivity, cGQDs are coupled to the CNT surface via the poly-l-lysine (PLL). After that, amino groups-riched PMB, which is highly specific for ET, is covalently bound to the surface of cGQDs. Three-dimensional spherical construction of cGQDs greatly benefits the loading effectiveness of the probe, and the detection sensitivity of the ET receives a significant boost accordingly. Compared with the LAL test (Fig. 1A), the PMB-modified CNTs FET biosensor is easy to operate and cost-effective. Moreover, this method has greater detection sensibility and resistance to interference, exhibiting the power and value for functional application in complex biological samples.

3,3-Dimethoxy-2,15,18-trioxa-3-silanonadecane solution and poly-l-lysine (PLL) were provided by Macklin (Shanghai, China); The polycarbazole sorted CNTs were provided by Peking University; carboxylated graphene quantum dots (cGQD) was purchased from Xianfeng Nano (Nanjing, China); Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China) provided N- hydroxysulfosuccinimide (sulfo-NHS, 98.5%) and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 98%) and glutaraldehyde (GA); polymyxin B (PMB) and endotoxin (Escherichia coli O55:B5) were purchased from Dalian Meilun Biologicals (Dalian, China); LAL Reagent Water (LRW, endotoxin content <0.001 EU/mL) and endotoxin test kit was purchased from BIOENDO (Xiamen, China); silanized glass sample bottles purchased from Agilent (Shanghai, China).
Healthy and bacterially infected serum samples were obtained from General Hospital of Central Theater Command, PLA (Wuhan, China). All clinical samples were approved by the hospital ethics committee.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of cGQD-modified CNTs FET devices
ce:section.ce:para: The FET chip was produced by standard semiconductor technology, and the cGQD-modified CNTs FET biosensor was obtained by the following steps. Briefly, 1 μL of 3,3-Dimethoxy-2,15,18-trioxa-3-silanonadecane solution was injected into the sensing channel of the device. Such a procedure changed the SiO2 covering from hydrophilic to hydrophobic, helping assembly of the hydrophobic CNTs onto the chip. The original concentration of CNTs stock solution was 20 μg/mL, which was further diluted to 10 μg/mL in toluene. Then 10 μL of CNTs were dropcasted on the sensing channel and allowed for reaction at ambient temperature for 30 min. The chip was sequentially soaked in toluene, 0.1% v/v trifluoroacetic acid in toluene by shaking for 2 min, and finally washed down with LRW and dehydrated with nitrogen. Stabilization of CNT deposition was carried out by annealing the chip at 200 °C for 20 min. The above procedure was repeated again to obtain CNT with higher homogeneity and stability. 0.01 mg/mL PLL was then added dropwise to the CNTs FET surface for connecting the CNTs and cGQDs. To obtain cGQDs-modified CNT FETs, the carboxyl group of cGDQ was firstly revitalized by a mixture of 0.4 M EDC and 0.1 M NHS at chamber temperature for 30 min. Subsequently, 10 μL of the revitalized cGQDs solution was added a drop to the CNTs FET sensing channel and reacted for 3 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent binding of PMB with cGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: PMB was firstly diluted to 10 μg/mL, and 10 μL of the diluted PMB was added droplets to the chip channel surface and combined at 37 °C for 2 h. Then the chip was flushed repeatedly with LRW to remove the unreacted PMB. Finally, BSA was added to the chip surface to reduce non-specific reactions, and then rinsed with LRW.

To avoid ET adsorb on the bottle wall and aggregate in the solution, the ET solution needed to be formulated in a silanized glass vial and incubated and vortexed at 37 °C for at least 30 min prior to usage. Then 10 μL of ET standard solution (10 fg/mL-1 ng/mL) or clinical serum sample to be tested were added dropwise onto the sensing channel, respectively, and allowed for incubation at 37 °C for 1 h, after which the chip was washed out with LRW and dried with nitrogen for next electrical test.

The measurement system used in this work consisted of a semiconductor parameter analyzer (Keithley 4200-SCS) and a probe station (EverBeing BD-6). The electrical properties of all FET facilities were tested at ambient temperature with a liquid gate. The I ds -Vg transfer characteristic curve was recorded by scanning the electrolyte Vg under the condition that Vds was fixed at 0.1 V (Ids is the source-drain current, Vg is the gate voltage, Vds is the source-drain voltage). In the real-time sensing experiment, Vds was fixed at 0.1 V and Vg was fixed at −0.3 V, and the rate of change of current (ΔI/I0) was considered as the normalized current response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of cGQDs-functionalized CNTs FET
ce:section.ce:para: The optical images of the FET chips for biosensing detection are shown in Fig. S1A-B. Each chip consists of six adjacent channels, where a single channel is 4 μm wide. The construction process of the cGQDs-functionalized CNTs FET biosensor is displayed in Fig. 2 A, in which the deposition of cGQDs and the modification of PMB on the CNTs FET chip are illustrated. Firstly, a layer of PLL is modified on the CNT surface via the cation-π interactions between PLL and CNT (Fig. 2A, left), which provides an effective interface to overcome the chemical inertness of the CNTs surface. Pre-activation of the cGQDs allows for covalent binding between PLL and cGQDs, enabling cGQDs to be bound to the PLL-CNTs FET surface (Fig. 2A, middle), subsequently the amino-riched PMB can further be immobilized onto the CNTs FET by covalently binding to cGQDs (Fig. 2A, right). To demonstrate the successful modification process, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), electrical measurement by semiconductor analyzer, and contact angle measurement were used for characterization. From the SEM in Fig. 2B, It could be seen that each CNTs FET chip contained 6 sensing channels (a), the source and drain were connected by high-purity polycarbazole-sorted CNTs (b), and the CNTs were uniformly dispersed in random orientation across the channels (c). Then, the successful binding of PLL to CNTs was verified by the content change of N elements in XPS before and after PLL modification, as shown in Fig. 2C. The original CNTs FET showed a small N1s peak at 399.8 eV because each polycarbazole molecule has one N atom [35], After PLL was modified, the drastic increase in the N1s peak demonstrated that the amino groups-enriched PLL was successfully modified [36]. The same conclusion was obtained from Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S2B). The peaks specific to carbon-based materials (D and G peaks) were shown in the original CNTs FET and the PLL-modified CNTs FET. Moreover, the intensity of the D peaks were both weak, indicating that the amorphous carbon content in the CNTs before and after PLL modification was very low and there were few defects. In addition, there was no noticeable shift (1595 cm−1) in the G-peak position before and after PLL modification. However, the intensity of the G-peak became feeble after the modification, verifying the existence of the cation-π interactions between PLL and CNT [37]. The contact angle was further used to test the hydrophilicity of the chip surface, as seen in Fig. S2A. After AMP (the AMP solution was prepared with H2O, NH4OH, and H2O2) soaking, the impurity particles on the chip surface were removed and a chemical oxide layer was formed showing hydrophilicity [38] (left, contact angle is 66.5°). After depositing hydrophobic CNTs, the surface became hydrophobic [39] (middle, contact angle is 94.4°). After modification with hydrophilic PLL [40], the contact angle became 75.7° (right). All characterizations indicate the successful modification of PLL. The subsequent TEM image demonstrated that the cGQDs covalently linked to PLL were uniformly dispersed in the solution (Fig. 2D), which is generally coincide with the results reported in the literatures [34,41]. Fig. 2E showed that the particle size of cGQDs was primarily dispersed in the range of 3–5 nm. Subsequently, to investigate the binding between cGQDs and PMB, the fluorescence spectra of the same concentration of cGQDs and cGQDs-PMB at 365 nm excitation wavelength were compared after PMB was bound with cGQDs. It was found that the fluorescence signal of cGQDs-PMB was lower than that of cGQDs as seen in Fig. S2C. This is probably due to the quenching of the fluorescence of cGQDs after PMB binding [42,43]. Finally, the electrical signals of each functionalization step were measured by a semiconductor analyzer. The Id-Vg curve in Fig. 2E further confirmed the successful construction of the PMB-modified CNTs FET biosensor. The prepared CNTs FET is P-type device, which is mainly conducted by hole carriers [26]. When the amino-rich PLL was modified, a large amount of positive charge was introduced into the sensing interface, resulting in an electrostatic gating effect that led to a growth of electron content in the channel [27,28], contributing to a decrease in current and a negative shift in the transfer curve. After cGQDs were combined to PLL, the introduction of massive number of negative charges caused an increase in hole carriers in the channel, which was manifested by a rise in current and a positive shift in the transfer curve. Since PMB contain amino groups, when they further bound to the CNTs FET surface via cGQDs, positive charges were introduced, leading to an increase of electrons in the channel and causing a decrease in current.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of cGQDs-functionalized CNTs FET sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The consideration that the select of experimental states affects the sensitivity of the assay in a large part, some related conditions were firstly optimized, containing the concentration and bonding time of cGQDs, the concentration of PMB and the combining time of ET (Fig. S3 A-D). To acquire the sufficient amount of cGQDs deposition, the concentration and sedimentation time of cGQDs were optimized and the most appropriate incubation time of cGQDs was found to be 3 h and the suitable concentration was 0.5 mg/mL. The concentration of PMB and the response time of target ET have an effect on the combining efficiency of target ET. These two parameters were further touched and it was found that 10 μg/mL of PMB and ET incubated for 1 h could derive the most pronounced signal response.

Based on the optimal conditions, as described above, a series of known concentrations of ET (10 fg/mL-1 ng/mL) were prepared and added dropwise to the cGQDs-functionalized CNTs FET sensor, and the corresponding Id-Vg transfer characteristic curve was recorded. As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, with the multiplicative increase in the concentration of ET standard solution, the current shifted proportionally in a positive direction. Since pI of ET is 3.1, it is negatively charged [44]. When binding of ET to the PMB-functionalized CNTs FET sensor, accumulation of negative charges were induced, resulting in an increase in hole carriers at the channel by p-type electrostatic doping. Thus, the transfer characteristic curves were proportional to the proactively shift, The results are in line with those published in the literatures [26,28]. To further quantify the electrical response of the sensor to detect ET, the change in Ids (ΔIds) before and after the addition of the ET was calculated. The calibration curve plotted in Fig. 3B showcased the fabulous linear correlation between the logarithm of the concentration and the response signal (R2 = 0.997). The linear regression equation was written as ΔIds = 1.8843lgCET+27.7310. The LOD was calculated to be approximately 4.6 fg/mL based on the triple signal-to-noise ratio.

The main role of cGQDs on the CNTs/PLL surface is to increase the specific surface area because the cGQDs are nanoparticles with a three-dimensional spherical structure, compared to the planar surface structure. With cGQDs modified on the CNT FET sensor, the capture probe PMB can effectively be immobilized, and bind with more targets, thus improving the detection sensitivity. In order to prove that the high sensitivity is caused by the cGQDs-modified CNT FET sensor, a planar surface other than cGQDs interface was employed, in which glutaraldehyde (GA) was used as a linker molecule to immobilize PMB on the PLL surface. In the experiment, 10 μL of GA solution (2.5%) was added onto the PLL-modified FET surface and incubated on the chip for 2 h, after which the same concentration of PMB was immobilized on the chip, since both PMB and PLL surfaces contain amino groups. As shown in Fig. S4, a series of known concentrations of ET (10 fg/mL-1 ng/mL) were added dropwise to the GA-functionalized CNTs FET sensor, and the corresponding Id-Vg transfer characteristic curves were recorded. As discussed above, the LOD was calculated to be approximately 173.9 fg/mL, which is 2 orders of magnitude higher than that obtained with the spherical cGQDs. The results reveal that cGQDs are three-dimensional spherical structures, which can expand the surface area and improve the detection sensitivity.

The detection properties of the cGQDs-modified CNTs FET in comparison with that of other sensors were summarized in Table S1. It was noteworthy that the constructed cGQDs-modified CNTs FET obtained a lower limit of detection, which was in the equivalent magnitude as that displayed in the electrochemical aptamer sensor (LOD is 8.7 fg/mL) [45]. In this electrochemical assay, the authors used the three-way DNA junction-aided enzymatic recycling and graphene nanohybrid for amplification, while a label-free and direct detection was used for the cGQDs-modified CNTs FET sensor. In view of local bacterial infections or bloodstream bacterial infections can trigger varying degrees of elevated ET levels in the body, and very limited amounts of ET can arise severe febrile reactions [46], the developed CNTs FET sensor assay promises to be a prospective weapon for clinical detection of low concentrations of ET.

In consideration of the more sophisticated ingredients of biological samples, it is also worthwhile to explore the competence of the as-prepared CNTs FET sensor. Several interfering substances including glycans (β-glucan, d-galactose), amino acids (l-cysteine, glycine), and ATP were chosen to interact with the PMB-modified CNTs FET sensor. Meanwhile, 1 × PBS was used as a blank control group. The electrical response of the PMB-modified CNTs FET sensor to various substances was shown in Fig. 3C and D. Negligible electrical signal change was observed after the addition of PBS and 100 pg/mL of the above-mentioned interfering substances, while the addition of 100 fg/mL of ET produced a significant electrical signal change. The specific combination reaction of PMB and ET caused a dramatically altered current [47]. The results show that the cGQDs-modified CNTs FET sensor has satisfactory detection selectivity and anti-interference capability, thus showing the capacity to be used for the detection of sophisticated biological substrates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the PMB-functionalized CNTs FET sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Since the chip surface needs to be repeatedly rinsed throughout the whole measurement process, the stability of the device is another vital capability that worth examined. We first investigated the stability over a short period of time by applying 1 × PBS dropwise to the constructed PMB-modified CNTs FET chip channel and then incubating for 6 h. The electrical signals were analyzed every 1 h. As seen in Fig. S5 A-B, the electrical sigals remained above 96% even after 6 h of incubation. Then, the longer stability of the device was further performed by storing the cGQDs-functionalized CNTs FET in a vacuum oven for 7 days, surveying the electrical signals and drawing the changes of current every other day. As shown in Fig. S5 C-D, after 7 days, the electrical signals could still maintain at about 90%, indicating good stability of the as-prepared biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of endotoxin in simulation samples
ce:section.ce:para: To be certain that the sensor has practical applicability, the cGQDs-modified CNTs FET was utilized to visually examine ET content in simulated biological samples. To emulate a clinical sample of an actual bacterial infections, a range of various content level of ET (100 fg/mL-1 ng/mL) were titrated into undiluted healthy human serum. In the situation without target, the similar volume of undiluted healthy standard serum was served as a blank control. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, the electrical signals presented a concentration-dependent increment, with the level of the target ET enhancing from 100 fg/mL to 1 ng/mL, which was manifested by a progressive positive movement in current. Excellent linearity between the change in current (ΔIds) and the logarithm of the concentration of ET, the regression equation was formulated as ΔIds = 1.8025lgCET+26.7770, while taking into account the greater than 3-fold signal-to-noise ratio, the LOD was calculated as 30.3 fg/mL. A more detailed representation is given in Fig. 4C, where we can see that the electrical signal variation generated in healthy human serum in the presence of the same concentration of target is weaker than that in PBS. The conceivable reason would be that the components in the emulated serum are more complicated and to a small extent provoke some disturbance. Meanwhile, to validate to a deeper extent whether the constructed cGQDs-functionalized CNTs FET sensor has the same power to monitor ET in real time in complex biological samples, the normalized current response was recorded. Briefly, after the baseline stabilization, healthy serum as a control sample, and ΕΤ with various concentration from 100 fg/mL to 1 ng/mL, were injected sequentially. As can be seen in Fig. 4D, addition of healthy serum to the sensor induced negligible current change, while addition of ET caused significant current change. The higher the concentration of ET, the more rise the current. These outcomes indicated that the consructed sensor has superior immunity to interruptions and can be adapted to further applications for real-time verification of multiple samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of clinical samples using the PMB-modified CNTs FET
ce:section.ce:para: The outstanding performance of a recommended nanosensor must ultimately be adapted to the needs of clinical examination. The practicability of the constructed device and the proposed strategy was proven by examining ET in actual clinical blood samples. Serum samples from 20 patients identified with blood Gram-negative bacterial infections and 10 healthy humans were appended to the cGQDs-modified CNTs FET sensor, respectively. The ΔIds was extracted for statistical analysis based on the Id-Vg curves. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the electrical signals of 20 Gram-negative bacterial infected serum samples were significantly higher than those of 10 healthy serum samples, and were statistically different (P < 0.001). As presented in the heat map in Fig. 5B, the signal responses were very strong in Gram-negative bacterial infections serum samples while the signal changes were relatively weak in healthy serum, which also demonstrating a noticeable differentiation between the two groupings. At the same time, the conventional method, LAL test, was also used to detect ET in these 30 clinical serum samples. The correlation analysis of the detection results of the two methods was performed. From Fig. 5C, it can be evidenced that there was a well-established linear correlation between the detection signals accessed from the constructed CNTs FET sensor and those obtained from the LAL test (Pearson's r = 0.96). The response of the heat map of the LAL test results in Fig. S6 was also very similar to that shown in Fig. 5B. Furthermore, to examine the clinical diagnostic performance of the CNTs FET sensor in more details, receiver operating characteristic curves (ROC) was plotted, and the outstanding accuracy was illustrated in Fig. 5D. The area under the curve (AUC) given from the CNTs FET sensor was even better than 0.9, which meets the criteria for reliable diagnosis (AUC >0.8). Up to this point, it can be summarized that the proposed method has the capabilities of detecting ET in clinical samples with superlative accuracy and reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time measurement of endotoxin in clinical samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further realize rapid detection of ET, the PMB-modified CNTs FET sensor was used to perform real-time dynamic monitoring of clinical Gram-negative bacterial infections blood samples. The detection schematic is shown in Fig. 6 A. Briefly, serum samples are injected directly to the channel of PMB-modified CNTs FET and then the electrical response is recorded in real time. As shown in Fig. 6 B-D, after waiting for the baseline was stabilized, the healthy serum samples were injected, all 3 sensors showed negligible response, however, after injection of infected serum samples, the sensors responded rapidly within 3 min. Meanwhile, since the various levels of ET in the patient serums, incorporation of ET to the sensor surface generated distinguishable current rise. The histogram in the Inset shows a distinct response differentiation between healthy sample and patients’ samples. In this analysis mode, by monitoring the change of electrical current in real time, the constructed PMB-modified CNTs FET biosensor can distinguish the ET level in serum samples within 3 min, thus determining the presence of Gram-negative bacterial infestation. The developed sensor is expected to provide an earlier and faster detection of disease for patients with syndromes such as endotoxemia and unexplained fever.

In this work, the cGQDs-modified CNTs FET sensor has achieved a certain degree of leap forward in the field of ET detection, as evidenced by short detection times, low detection limits and high accuracy. Impressively, this sensing platform uses the cGQDs nanoparticles to expend the active area and load more capture probes, from which realizing a quantum leap in detection sensitivity. Thus, the cGQDs-functionalized CNTs FET sensor enables detect ET with high sensitivity capability in sophisticated matrices with a detection limit of 30.3 fg/mL. Meanwhile, the cGQDs-functionalized CNTs FET sensor can detect ET in serum samples from clinically Gram-negative bacterial infections and can distinguish from healthy individuals with convincing accuracy (the AUC of ROC curve is 0.99). More importantly, the sensor is able to monitor the ET level in clinical samples in real time and deliver relevant results within 3 min. These remarkable device performance and detection capabilities endow the sensor with potential clinical applications.

Q. C. performed technology development, analytical characterization, performance measurement and data treatment. J-H. L. and T-X. L. helped to fabricate the devices. Y-T.L, L-N.T helped with ideas for lines of text. K. L helped to characterize cGQDs modification. X–H.C., Z-Y. Z. and G-J. Z. conceived and supervised the project. G.-J. Z. was in charge of supervision, project administration, writing-review & editing, and funding acquisition. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974035).
